mechanism_description: Adopting sustainable forest management practices increases forest carbon storage and reduces land-use emissions, supporting a shift of the sector toward a net carbon sink.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem enhancement (forest management) → ↑ forest carbon stocks + ↓ land-use CO₂ emissions → ↓ net atmospheric CO₂
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Forest governance and operational practices (e.g., reduced-impact logging, longer rotation periods, protection of high-carbon areas, improved monitoring/set, reduced degradation and deforestation).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Increased biomass and soil carbon retention/accumulation and reduced carbon release from tree removal, burning, drainage, and degradation; improved forest regeneration and reduced disturbance intensity.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Higher net carbon sequestration and lower land-use CO₂ (and some non-CO₂) emissions, shifting forests toward a net carbon sink.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increased net CO₂ removal/avoidance through higher forest carbon stocks and reduced forest-related emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing forests to increase/maintain ecosystem carbon stocks and avoid emissions directly affects atmospheric CO₂ via land carbon sinks and reduced land-use emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While SFM can deliver resource efficiency (more wood per impact) and adaptation co-benefits (reduced wildfire/erosion risk), the dominant climate-relevant effect across this mechanism is ecosystem-driven carbon sequestration and emissions avoidance—best captured as Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If “sustainable forest management” were implemented mainly to increase timber yield or substitute wood for carbon-intensive materials, the dominant mechanism could shift toward resource efficiency/mitigation via substitution; however, that is a different canonical mechanism than explicit forest carbon sequestration/emissions reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that fits this canonical mechanism necessarily operates through forest ecosystem carbon stock enhancement and/or avoided forest emissions, so the primary category remains Nature-Based Solutions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any further subtype (e.g., avoided deforestation vs. improved forest management vs. restoration) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity if “SFM” is pursued primarily for timber productivity, bioenergy feedstocks, or material substitution; those would require a different mechanism focus. Also, benefits can be reversed by leakage, weak enforcement, or disturbance (fire/pests), but the intended dominant pathway remains ecosystem carbon.
key_indicators: “turn it into net sink by 2050 / by 2030”; “depends primarily on the success of… actions”; “adopting sustainable forest management practices”; “reduce the emission of this sector”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; watershed/soil protection; improved livelihoods for forest-dependent communities; reduced erosion and fire risk
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via regulatory mandates or certification/procurement requirements, but the excerpt does not specify an enforceable standard or instrument—only an intended action—so it is treated as a planning/strategy commitment.
